result.markdown:
---
title: "Regression Splines: An Interactive Introduction"
subtitle: "Modelling nonlinear age effects in clinical data"
format: live-html
engine: knitr
webr:
  packages:
    - ggplot2
    - splines
---




## The problem

Many clinical predictors do not relate linearly to the outcome. A textbook
example is **age**: systolic blood pressure rises gently in young adulthood,
accelerates around midlife, and may flatten in the very old.

Let's simulate a dataset that captures this pattern and compare three
modelling strategies — all running **inside your browser**.




::: {.cell autorun='true'}

```{.webr .cell-code}
library(ggplot2)
library(splines)

set.seed(2026)
n   <- 400
age <- runif(n, 20, 85)
# True nonlinear mean
mu  <- 110 + 0.10 * age + 0.012 * (age - 20)^2 - 0.0001 * (age - 20)^3
sbp <- mu + rnorm(n, 0, 8)
clin <- data.frame(age = age, sbp = sbp)

ggplot(clin, aes(age, sbp)) +
  geom_point(alpha = 0.5) +
  labs(x = "Age (years)", y = "Systolic BP (mmHg)") +
  theme_minimal()
```
:::




## Attempt 1 — a straight line




::: {.cell autorun='true'}

```{.webr .cell-code}
m_lin <- lm(sbp ~ age, data = clin)

ggplot(clin, aes(age, sbp)) +
  geom_point(alpha = 0.4) +
  geom_smooth(method = "lm", se = TRUE, colour = "steelblue") +
  labs(title = sprintf("Linear fit | R² = %.3f", summary(m_lin)$r.squared)) +
  theme_minimal()
```
:::




The residuals are systematically positive in the middle and negative at the
tails — a clear misspecification signal.

## Attempt 2 — polynomials (editable)

A common quick fix is to add higher-order terms. **Edit the chunk below**:
change `degree` from 3 to 7, 10, 15. Watch the curve oscillate at the
boundaries — Runge's phenomenon in action.




::: {.cell}

```{.webr .cell-code}
degree <- 3   # <-- change me, then click Run

m_poly <- lm(sbp ~ poly(age, degree), data = clin)

ggplot(clin, aes(age, sbp)) +
  geom_point(alpha = 0.4) +
  geom_smooth(method = "lm",
              formula = y ~ poly(x, degree),
              se = TRUE, colour = "darkorange") +
  labs(title = sprintf("Polynomial degree %d", degree)) +
  theme_minimal()
```
:::




The pedagogical point: polynomials are *global* — a single tail observation
can yank the entire curve.

## Attempt 3 — natural cubic splines (reactive)

Splines are *local*: piecewise polynomials joined smoothly at knots.
Drag the slider to change the degrees of freedom.




```{ojs}
//| echo: false
viewof df_val = Inputs.range(
  [3, 20],
  { value: 5, step: 1, label: "Spline df:" }
)
```

::: {.cell autorun='true' input='df_val'}

```{.webr .cell-code}
m_ns <- lm(sbp ~ ns(age, df = df_val), data = clin)

age_grid <- data.frame(age = seq(min(clin$age), max(clin$age), length.out = 200))
pred     <- predict(m_ns, newdata = age_grid, interval = "confidence")
plot_df  <- cbind(age_grid, pred)

ggplot(clin, aes(age, sbp)) +
  geom_point(alpha = 0.4) +
  geom_ribbon(data = plot_df,
              aes(y = fit, ymin = lwr, ymax = upr),
              alpha = 0.2, fill = "darkgreen") +
  geom_line(data = plot_df,
            aes(y = fit),
            colour = "darkgreen", linewidth = 1) +
  labs(title = sprintf("Natural cubic spline, df = %d", df_val),
       x = "Age (years)", y = "Systolic BP (mmHg)") +
  theme_minimal()
```
:::




## What just happened

Everything above — data simulation, `lm()` fitting, prediction with
confidence bands, the `ggplot2` rendering, and the reactive update when you
move the slider — executed **on your laptop, inside the browser tab**.
There is no R server. The R interpreter itself was compiled to WebAssembly
by the [WebR project](https://docs.r-wasm.org/webr/latest/) and shipped as
static files alongside this page.

The implication for teaching: students can break the model. Push df to 20
and watch it overfit; drop it to 3 and watch it underfit. Edit the
polynomial degree and watch the boundaries explode. No installations, no
hosted Shiny server, no IT request, no GDPR question about cloud compute.

## Things to try

- Add `geom_rug(sides = "b")` to show data density and rationalise knot placement.
- Replace `ns()` with `bs()` and compare boundary behaviour — discuss why
  natural splines are usually preferred for clinical age effects.
- Add a second slider for the random seed; you'll see fits with df = 18
  are wildly different across simulations, df = 5 is stable. That **is**
  the bias–variance tradeoff, made visible.
- Drop a brief discussion of penalised splines (`mgcv::s()`) as the natural
  next step for the aggregation lecture.
